skillName: Angles in a quadrilateral sum to 360°
misconceptionSummary: Students forget that the four interior angles of a quadrilateral always add up to 360° and guess or use 180° instead.

visuals.ruleText: The four interior angles of any quadrilateral add up to 360°
visuals.subText: Works for squares, rectangles, parallelograms, trapezoids, and irregular quadrilaterals

steps.narration: Every quadrilateral — any four-sided shape — has interior angles summing to three hundred and sixty degrees. This includes squares, rectangles, parallelograms, trapezoids, and irregular four-sided shapes. Write the equation: all four angles equal three hundred and sixty.

Quadrilateral with angles ninety, eighty, one hundred and ten, and x. Sum: ninety plus eighty plus one hundred and ten is two hundred and eighty. X equals three hundred and sixty minus two hundred and eighty, which is eighty degrees.

lines.text: 4 angles needed — count sides before solving
All four must be interior angles of the shape

steps.narration: Check: ninety plus eighty plus one hundred and ten plus eighty equals three hundred and sixty. Count four sides before setting up the equation — make sure you have four angles, all interior.

visuals.label: Four interior angles, sum = 360° — subtract three known angles from 360°

steps.narration: Eighty degrees. Any quadrilateral, four angles, sum to three hundred and sixty. Subtract the three known angles from three hundred and sixty to find the fourth.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: using 180° for quadrilateral angles, giving a negative or wrong answer. Quadrilaterals have four angles summing to 360°. Subtract three known angles from 360° to find the fourth.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Quadrilateral angles sum to 360°, not 180°. 180° is for triangles. Write the equation with all four angles equalling 360°, then subtract the three you know.
